file_name: Bouzon et al. - 2025 - MetaBlock-SE A Method to Deal With Missing Metadata in Multimodal Skin Cancer Classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes MetaBlock-SE, a deep learning method for multimodal skin cancer classification. It integrates a sentence embedding algorithm (using AlbertTokenizerFast) to process patient metadata and combines it with imaging data using a ResNet-50 backbone (a Convolutional Neural Network) to classify skin lesions (BCC, SCC, MEL, etc.) on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel method called MetaBlock-SE to address missing metadata in multimodal skin cancer classification. It details the architecture (integrating SBERT sentence embeddings with a ResNet-50 backbone), describes experimental setups on the PAD-UFES-20 and an extended dataset, and reports original results including balanced accuracy metrics (70.2 ± 2.8 and 68.2 ± 1.0) and statistical comparisons (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). The structure includes Introduction, Methods, Experiments, Results, and Discussion, indicating it is an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: MetaBlock-SE: A Method to Deal With Missing Metadata in Multimodal Skin Cancer Classification
journal: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of missing metadata in multimodal skin cancer classification by proposing MetaBlock-SE, an extension of the MetaBlock architecture that integrates sentence embeddings to capture semantic relationships in patient data. The method combines these embeddings with imaging features using a ResNet-50 backbone to classify skin lesions into categories such as Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Nevus. The authors evaluated the model on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset and a newly extended version containing over 16,000 samples. Results demonstrated that MetaBlock-SE outperformed the original MetaBlock architecture, achieving balanced accuracies of 70.2 ± 2.8 and 68.2 ± 1.0 on the respective datasets. The proposed approach is noted for being computationally efficient and suitable for deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant lesion class included in the PAD-UFES-20 and extended PAD-UFES-20 datasets used for classification experiments.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Melanoma, and Nevus)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using multimodal data (clinical metadata and imaging). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using balanced accuracy, F1-Score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin lesion dataset composed of clinical images collected from smartphones, containing patient clinical metadata. It was used as the primary benchmark for evaluating the proposed MetaBlock-SE method under controlled conditions.

use.training: Fine-tuned on the target dataset for 100 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (implied by early stopping and learning rate reduction logic).
use.testing: Evaluated for balanced accuracy, F1-Score, and AUC to compare against original MetaBlock and vision models.
use.other: Used to simulate missing metadata rates from 0% to 70% to test robustness.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A new extended version of the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, seven times larger, collected in collaboration with the Dermatological and Surgical Assistance Program at the Federal University of Espírito Santo (UFES, Brazil) from 2019 to 2025. It serves low-income individuals in rural areas and contains real-world missing metadata.

use.training: Fine-tuned on the target dataset for 100 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Evaluated for balanced accuracy, F1-Score, and AUC to compare against MetaBlock, Cross-Attention, Bayesian Network, and vanilla CNN models in a real-world scenario with inherent missing metadata.
use.other: Used to demonstrate robustness to real-world missing metadata (e.g., ~31% missing elevation/growth info).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance using a stratified group scheme, ensuring stratification by label frequency while grouping by patient to prevent data leakage.
To statistically compare results across experiments and assess significant differences in performance between models (e.g., MetaBlock vs. MetaBlock-SE) under simulated missing metadata scenarios.
To report the uncertainty of the balanced accuracy performance metrics for the models across different missing metadata rates.

To rank the performance of different fusion methods (MetaBlock-SE, MetaBlock, Cross-Attention, Bayesian Network) based on balanced accuracy, using weights of 0.75 for average and 0.25 for standard deviation.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance between the compared models (e.g., MetaBlock-SE vs. MetaBlock, or MetaBlock-SE vs. Cross-Attention).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between the compared models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports p-values to determine statistical significance. For the comparison between MetaBlock-SE and MetaBlock on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, a p-value of 0.0136 was reported, indicating a statistically significant difference. For the comparison on the extended dataset, p-values were reported for Balanced Accuracy (0.784), AUC (0.076), and F1-Score (0.024) when comparing MetaBlock-SE to the Cross-Attention method. A p-value < 0.05 (e.g., 0.024 for F1-Score) indicates a significant difference, while p > 0.05 (e.g., 0.784 for BACC) indicates no significant difference.

PAD-UFES-20.experiment: Part 1: Simulating Missing Metadata (0% to 70% missing rates)

MetaBlock-SE.description: Outperformed original MetaBlock in all scenarios. Performance decreased as missing metadata rate increased, but remained more robust than MetaBlock.

MetaBlock.description: Outperformed vanilla CNN models when metadata was complete. Showed steeper decline in performance compared to MetaBlock-SE as missing metadata increased. Statistically significantly different from MetaBlock-SE at 20% missing metadata (p-value = 0.0136).

Vanilla CNNs.description: Outperformed by MetaBlock and MetaBlock-SE when metadata was well-structured. Performance comparable to MetaBlock-SE when dropout rate reached 50%.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: Evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. Primary metric was Balanced Accuracy. Wilcoxon signed-rank test used for statistical comparison.

Extended PAD-UFES-20.experiment: Part 2: Real-world Missing Metadata Case Study

MetaBlock-SE.comparison_vs_Cross_Attention: Similar BACC (p-value = 0.784), Similar AUC (p-value = 0.076), Higher F1-Score (p-value = 0.024)

Cross-Attention.description: Compared against MetaBlock-SE. No significant difference in BACC or AUC, but lower F1-Score.

Extended PAD-UFES-20.notes: Dataset contains 16,318 samples with inherent real-world missing metadata (e.g., ~31% missing elevation/growth info). MetaBlock-SE achieved top A-TOPSIS ranking. Model size increase was modest (43 MB) due to paraphrase-albert-small-v2.

architecture_used.main_model: MetaBlock-SE (an extension of the Metadata Processing Block architecture)
architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-trained weights for vision backbones (via torchvision API); paraphrase-albert-small-v2 for sentence embeddings; PubMedBERT also tested for sentence embeddings

architecture_details.metadata_extractor: Sentence-BERT (SBERT) model using paraphrase-albert-small-v2 to generate dense sentence embeddings (768 dimensions) from metadata features, replacing one-hot encoding
architecture_details.fusion_method: MetaBlock core equation using LSTM-like gates to scale and shift image feature maps based on metadata embeddings

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Exponential decay by factor of 0.1 if no improvement over 10 consecutive epochs
training_details.loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy loss (weights inversely proportional to class frequency)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: horizontal and vertical flipping
Data augmentation: addition of random noise
Metadata tokenization using AlbertTokenizerFast (vocabulary size 30,000, max input length 512, right-side padding and truncation)
Metadata organization as continuous sentences for SBERT input
Exclusion of ancestry information from extended dataset due to >70% missing values
Exclusion of lesion diameter features from extended dataset for consistency

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a standard geometric transform during training.
Applied as a color transform during training.
Applied as a color transform during training.
Applied as a color transform during training.
Applied as a geometric transform during training.
Applied as a noise-based augmentation technique during training.

conclusions: The proposed MetaBlock-SE method, which integrates sentence embeddings to handle missing metadata, outperforms the original MetaBlock architecture across all simulated and real-world missing metadata scenarios.
MetaBlock-SE achieved a balanced accuracy of 70.2 ± 2.8 on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset and 68.2 ± 1.0 on the extended PAD-UFES-20 dataset using the ResNet-50 backbone.

In real-world scenarios with inherent missing metadata, MetaBlock-SE achieved top ranking in A-TOPSIS analysis and demonstrated superior precision (F1-Score) compared to the Cross-Attention method, while maintaining similar balanced accuracy and AUC.
MetaBlock-SE consistently outperformed the original MetaBlock and Bayesian Network baselines across all vision backbones tested.

The method is computationally efficient, with a modest model size increase of 43 MB due to the paraphrase-albert-small-v2 embedding, making it suitable for deployment on mobile and edge-computing platforms in low-resource environments.

Performance of MetaBlock-SE decreases as the rate of missing metadata increases; specifically, it can become inferior to vision model baselines when the dropout rate reaches 50%, indicating it is not a complete solution for all missing data scenarios.

The study's generalizability is limited by the use of only the PAD-UFES-20 and its extended version, and further validation on datasets from different clinical settings is needed due to the scarcity of public skin cancer datasets with rich metadata.
important_remarks: The study is limited to two datasets (PAD-UFES-20 and an extended version) collected in Brazil, which may restrict generalizability to other demographic groups or clinical settings with different metadata structures.
The extended dataset serves low-income individuals in rural areas, potentially introducing socioeconomic and geographic bias not present in standard clinical cohorts.
Ancestry information was excluded from the extended dataset due to >70% missing values, limiting the model's ability to account for demographic risk factors.
Lesion diameter features were excluded from the extended dataset for consistency with the original PAD-UFES-20, despite their clinical relevance.

A Chi-square test revealed that missingness in the extended dataset is correlated with lesion type (p < 10^-12), indicating the data is not missing completely at random (MCAR), which complicates the interpretation of robustness.
Performance of MetaBlock-SE decreases significantly as missing metadata rates increase, becoming inferior to vision-only baselines when the dropout rate reaches 50%.
The implementation of the Bayesian Network baseline did not use K-Nearest Neighbors for imputation, differing from the original authors' approach, which may affect the fairness of the comparison.

The model was evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation with stratification by label frequency and grouping by patient to prevent data leakage, which is a strength in preventing overfitting to individual patients.
The study acknowledges that future work should include fine-tuning sentence embeddings with vision backbones, suggesting current results may be suboptimal compared to fully joint training.
